1: liwyātān nāḥāš bārîaḥ — "Leviathan, the fleeing serpent"; liwyātān nāḥāš ʿăqallātôn — "Leviathan, the twisting/coiling serpent"; hattannîn ʾăšer bayyām — "the dragon that is in the sea." These are the three names of the cosmic chaos-monster from Canaanite mythology (attested in Ugaritic texts as Lotan). YHWH's ḥereb qāšâ ûgĕdôlâ ûḥăzāqâ (hard, great, and strong sword) will slay it. The Day of the LORD inaugurates the defeat of cosmic chaos.
2: kerem ḥemed — "vineyard of delight/desire" (ḥemed, H2532, desirable, lovely). This vineyard is presented positively — in contrast to 5:1-7's vineyard that produced wild grapes and was destroyed. The shift is significant: the same vineyard imagery now pictures YHWH's relationship to his people as one of delight rather than disappointment. ʿannû lāh — "answer/sing about it" — the song is one of celebration.
3: ʾănî Yhwh nōṣĕrāh — "I, YHWH, am its keeper/guardian" (nāṣar, H5341, to guard, watch, preserve). The comparison with the withered vineyard of ch. 5 is deliberate: there YHWH removed his protection; here he personally provides continuous irrigation (lirgāʿîm ʾašqennāh, "moment by moment I water it") and guards it day and night (pen yipqōd ʿāleyhā).
4: ḥēmâ ʾên lî — "I have no wrath" — YHWH's posture toward his now-restored vineyard is one from which anger has departed. The only thing that might provoke his engagement is šāmîr wāšayit (briers and thorns, which represent external enemies or internal sin) — but even then he would march (ʾemdēhā) through them and burn them together (yāḥad ʾaṣṣîtennāh).
5: ô yĕḥāzeq bĕmaʿûzzî — "or let him take hold of my fortress/protection" — an alternative to being burned: seeking YHWH's protection by making peace (šālôm yaʿăśeh lî). The offer is open: yaʿăśeh šālôm lî — "let him make peace with me." This unexpected clemency in the middle of an oracle against the enemies of the vineyard models YHWH's willingness to receive any who turn to him.
6: hayyābāʾîm — "in coming days" (the plural of bāʾ, the ones coming/what is to come — a temporal expression for the eschatological future). Israel will blossom (yiprāḥ), sprout (ûparāḥ), and fill the face of the world (ûmillĕʾû pĕnê-ṯēḇēl tĕnûʾâ) with fruit. The agricultural imagery carries the eschatological restoration — the people of YHWH become fruitful in a way that blesses the entire inhabited world.
7: The rhetorical question: hakĕmakkat makkēhû hikkāhû — "has he (YHWH) struck him (Israel) as he struck those who struck him?" The answer is implied: no — YHWH's discipline of Israel, though real, is not the same as his judgment on Israel's enemies. ʾim-kĕhereḡ hărûgāyw hōrāg — "has he killed as those who killed him were killed?" The graduated distinction in severity of punishment is a statement of divine mercy within judgment.
8: bĕsaʾssēʾâ bĕšallĕḥāh tĕrîḇennāh — "by driving her away, by sending her away, you contended with her" (rîḇ, to contend at law, to dispute). The exile is framed as a legal dispute in which YHWH argues his case against his people. hāgâ bĕrûḥô haqqāšâ bĕyôm qādîm — "he drove her away with his harsh/east wind on a day of the east wind" — the east wind (qādîm) is the hot, destructive desert wind from the east, used as a metaphor for divine judgment (cf. Jer 18:17; Hos 13:15).
9: lākēn bĕzōʾt yĕkuppār ʿăwōn-yaʿăqōb — "therefore by this will the iniquity of Jacob be covered/atoned for" (kāpar, H3722, to cover, to make atonement). The instrument of atonement is the removal of sin — specifically the destruction of the altars and Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm) and incense altars (ḥammānîm) that represented the sin. The atonement is enacted through the exile's purification of idolatry from the people.
10: The fortified city (ʿîr bĕṣûrâ) is desolate (bāḏāḏ) — an image of Samaria or Jerusalem after conquest. nĕwēh mĕšullāḥ — "an abandoned/deserted habitation" — the city that expelled its inhabitants is now itself deserted, its land serving only as pasture (wirʿâ šām ʿēgel). The calf grazing on the ruins of a great city is the ultimate image of civilization reduced to wilderness.
11: biyḇôš qĕṣîrāh tišbarnâ — "when its boughs are dry, they are broken off" (yāḇēš, dry/withered; šāḇar, break). The women come and kindle fire with the branches — the ruins become fuel. ʿam lōʾ-bînôt hûʾ — "it is a people without understanding/discernment" — the theological diagnosis that makes judgment inevitable. Without understanding of YHWH's ways, there is no path to blessing.
12: wĕhāyâ bayyôm hahûʾ yāḥbōṭ Yhwh — "on that day YHWH will beat out/thresh" (ḥābaṭ, H2251, to beat grain from stalks) — the gathering image. From the Euphrates River (nahar) to the Brook of Egypt (naḥal miṣrayim), YHWH will glean the scattered Israelites one by one (ʾeḥāḏ ʾeḥāḏ). The careful individual gathering is the opposite of mass deportation — YHWH brings his people home one at a time.
13: wĕhāyâ bayyôm hahûʾ yiqqāʿēʾ bĕšôpār gāḏôl — "on that day a great trumpet will be blown" (šôpār gāḏôl, the great ram's horn). The lost ones (hāʾōbĕḏîm) in Assyria and the scattered ones (hanniddāḥîm) in Egypt will come and worship (hištaḥăwû lYhwh) on the holy mountain in Jerusalem. The great trumpet of eschatological ingathering is developed in Matt 24:31 (the Son of Man's angels with a great trumpet) and 1 Cor 15:52 (the last trumpet at resurrection).
